a: The estimate removes work already automated in current POS, catalog, payment, and inventory systems.
a: Custom awards and collectibles are more advice-intensive than candles or replenishment supplies.

rho: Very small catalogs and highly customized products may not justify integration expense.
rho: Some tools improve assortment or service quality without reducing paid hours or avoidable hiring.

e: NAICS illustrative examples are not a weighted distribution of firms or receipts.
e: The 2022 code incorporates some former electronic-shopping and direct-selling establishments, increasing channel variation.

s5: No denominator-consistent deal-flow series exists publicly for this heterogeneous residual code.
s5: Inventory quality, supplier relationships, leases, and owner-held product expertise materially affect saleability.

q: Merchandise mix creates large differences in price transparency and differentiation.
q: This primitive excludes implementation success and channel volume loss.
d5: The historical sales series uses a broader older classification and is not price adjusted.
d5: Pandemic-era demand and subsequent normalization distort the five-year comparison.
d5: The retained merchandise categories may move in opposite directions, making an average highly composition-sensitive.
o: General retail e-commerce share is not the channel mix of the narrowed 459999 basket.

o: The lower bound reflects operator displacement and self-service, not underlying demand loss, which is reserved for d5.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer- and business-paid merchandise revenue at posted or quoted prices, with repeat replenishment and occasional customized orders; product selection, light design or configuration, fulfillment, and customer support are bundled into merchandise margin rather than separately billed.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 459999 combines small-ticket repeat retail, customized awards, seasonal regulated merchandise, cemetery memorials, home-security equipment, and high-ticket pool or hot-tub dealers. A single demand, workflow, and retention view would be incoherent, so the lens keeps the repeat-purchase and recurring custom-order specialty-retail model and excludes durable-project and regulated-seasonal models. The narrowing is based on business-model comparability, not attractiveness; e estimates the retained share of LMM firms.
lens.excluded_activities: Hot-tub and above-ground-pool equipment dealers dominated by infrequent high-ticket projects, cemetery memorial dealers, home-security equipment dealers, seasonal or regulated fireworks sellers, shells, captive outlets, financing vehicles, owner-only microshops below the EBITDA band, and firms without transferable operating systems or customer relationships.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market specialty retailers centered on repeat-purchase small-ticket merchandise or recurring custom orders, including art supplies, candles, new collectors' items, trophies and awards, artificial or dried flowers, and recurring pool or spa supplies, sold through stores or owned online storefronts to consumers and external business or institutional customers.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe retail salespersons and cashiers across all retail industries, not the narrowed 459999 segment or its wage-weighted occupation mix. I map product questions, recommendations, promotions, customer communication, order entry, reconciliation, and inventory administration to AI-assistable work, while discounting physical stocking, packaging, customization, visual judgment, and in-person selling. The proxy likely overstates exposure because generic task descriptions underrepresent the tactile and visual work in art supplies, collectibles, awards, flowers, and other specialty merchandise.
caveats: No public occupation distribution was found for the new six-digit code or the narrowed segment.
The estimate removes work already automated in current POS, catalog, payment, and inventory systems.
Custom awards and collectibles are more advice-intensive than candles or replenishment supplies.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupation roster and four-week task diary from at least twenty narrowed-lens retailers, segmented by sales, customization, fulfillment, merchandising, purchasing, customer service, and administration and matched to existing POS and e-commerce automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The exposed work is concentrated in language, information, and routine transaction workflows: product discovery, quote and description drafting, support, promotions, order status, demand planning, reconciliation, and basic catalog maintenance. BLS also identifies payment, stocking, delivery, and inventory duties, while its Occupational Requirements Survey finds retail salespersons stand for most of the workday and commonly reach or use low postures. Those physical requirements, plus customized production and merchandise judgment, keep most current work outside direct AI substitution.

d5.bridge: The Census/FRED series measures nominal monthly sales for the broad pre-2022 miscellaneous-store category, including subindustries and product lines outside both 459999 and the narrowed lens, rather than constant-price quantity. Seasonally adjusted sales rose from $10.879 billion in January 2021 to $16.041 billion in March 2026. I treat this only as evidence against broad category collapse, remove most apparent growth for inflation, scope changes, and out-of-lens categories, and center the narrowed basket near flat real demand. The raw proxy likely overstates narrowed-lens constant-price demand because it is nominal and includes faster-growing unrelated miscellaneous retailers.
caveats: The historical sales series uses a broader older classification and is not price adjusted.
Pandemic-era demand and subsequent normalization distort the five-year comparison.
The retained merchandise categories may move in opposite directions, making an average highly composition-sensitive.
d5.change_evidence: Circana, NIQ, Numerator, or merchant-acquirer panels with five years of units and inflation-adjusted sales by the retained merchandise lines, split between specialty stores, owned e-commerce, marketplaces, and mass merchants, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Art, candle, collector, award, decorative-flower, and maintenance-supply demand is discretionary and category-specific, with no single structural growth engine. Broad miscellaneous-store sales have expanded in nominal terms, but the category is wider than this lens and the source is not price adjusted. The five-year base therefore assumes roughly stable real demand, with wide bounds for consumer spending, hobby participation, institutional awards, housing-linked pool supplies, and online channel shift.

caveats: The supplied LMM firm-count primitive is a declared dataset gap, so this share cannot yield a defensible eligible-firm count.
NAICS illustrative examples are not a weighted distribution of firms or receipts.
The 2022 code incorporates some former electronic-shopping and direct-selling establishments, increasing channel variation.
e.change_evidence: Six-digit business-register or SUSB microdata classified by primary merchandise line, combined with broker and lender files showing normalized EBITDA, revenue recurrence, owner dependence, lease assignability, and operating-system maturity, would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The official definition lists ten very different examples. The narrowed lens keeps the repeat-oriented small-ticket or custom-order categories and recurring supplies, while excluding durable projects and regulated seasonal retail. Because example lists provide no firm weights, one-half is a neutral center with a wide range. Additional exclusions apply for owner-dependent boutiques, nontransferable leases or supplier rights, and firms lacking normalized LMM economics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: General retail e-commerce share is not the channel mix of the narrowed 459999 basket.
An online transaction still requires an accountable merchant, but it may not be a lens operator or current store owner.
The lower bound reflects operator displacement and self-service, not underlying demand loss, which is reserved for d5.
o.change_evidence: Merchandise-line channel-share cohorts through 2031, including marketplace seller identity, direct-brand penetration, store closures, self-checkout use, customization incidence, returns, and the share of orders fulfilled by independent specialty operators, would materially change the range.
o.rationale: Demand can migrate from stores to marketplaces, direct-to-consumer brands, self-checkout, and online self-service, but software alone does not source, own, customize, merchandise, fulfill, warrant, and accept returns on physical products. Census reported e-commerce at 16.9% of total U.S. retail sales in the first quarter of 2026 and growing faster year over year than total retail. Small, shippable products in this lens may be more exposed than retail overall, while custom awards, local urgency, advice, and pickup preserve an accountable operator role.

caveats: No causal evidence was found for five-year retention of AI labor savings in the narrowed segment.
Merchandise mix creates large differences in price transparency and differentiation.
This primitive excludes implementation success and channel volume loss.
q.change_evidence: SKU- and order-level gross-margin panels before and after automation, matched against online and local competitor prices, promotion depth, supplier rebates, return rates, repeat-customer retention, and customization mix, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Retailers set posted or quoted merchandise prices rather than operating under cost-plus pass-through contracts, so they can initially retain labor savings. Over five years, searchable SKU prices, online marketplaces, promotions, and low switching costs should transfer part of the gain to customers. Retention is stronger for proprietary assortments, urgent local availability, trusted curation, and customized trophies or awards than for commodity candles, supplies, and widely available collectibles.

caveats: This is a bounded implementation judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
Very small catalogs and highly customized products may not justify integration expense.
Some tools improve assortment or service quality without reducing paid hours or avoidable hiring.
rho.change_evidence: Controlled pilots across representative art-supply, candle, collectibles, trophy, artificial-flower, and pool-supply operators, reporting paid hours, order errors, returns, conversion, shrink, customer escalations, and persistence for twelve months, would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Common cloud retail tools should make product-content, marketing, support, purchasing, scheduling, and reporting improvements implementable within five years. BLS expects cashier employment to decline as self-service checkout and online sales advance, demonstrating a feasible path for transaction automation. Realization remains below full exposure because small retailers often have weak item data, fragmented systems, low change capacity, and many exceptions; customer-facing errors and inaccurate product or customization advice require review.

s5.bridge: The Census Annual Business Survey statistic covers responding owners of 4.1 million U.S. employer businesses in 2018, not narrowed-lens 459999 firms, and measures owner age rather than qualifying control transfers. I use the 51% age-55-plus share as a succession-pressure anchor and reduce substantially for delayed retirement, closure, family or employee succession without a qualifying external transfer, and businesses that cannot sell as going concerns. The proxy likely overstates qualifying transfers because age does not establish a sale decision or transferability.
caveats: The owner-age source is economy-wide, dated 2018, and counts responding owners rather than firms.
No denominator-consistent deal-flow series exists publicly for this heterogeneous residual code.
Inventory quality, supplier relationships, leases, and owner-held product expertise materially affect saleability.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of industry- and merchandise-tagged closed transactions from BizBuySell, DealStats, Axial, SBA 7(a) acquisition loans, and specialty retail brokers, divided by a consistent stock of eligible LMM firms and classified by external control sale versus closure or internal transfer, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: An owner-operated specialty-retail population plausibly has meaningful five-year succession activity, but direct 459999 deal incidence was not found. Census evidence that 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least 55 supports a high-teens center while the broad range recognizes that many exits are delayed, internal, or closures.

principal_driver: Repeat transactions and standardized catalog, support, purchasing, and order workflows create the main implementable labor opportunity.
principal_weakness: The residual code's product heterogeneity and missing eligible-firm denominator make population-level inference unusually fragile.
